questions.question: Explain the three file allocation methods and state one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Contiguous allocation: consecutive blocks, fast access, suffers from external fragmentation
Linked allocation: blocks connected via pointers, no external fragmentation, poor random access
Indexed allocation: uses index block for pointers, fast random access but index block overhead

questions.question: Describe four techniques used for free space management in file systems.
keyPoints: Bit Vector (Bitmap): array of bits marking free/allocated blocks
Linked List: free blocks linked together in a list
Grouping: first free block stores addresses of k other free blocks
Counting: stores starting address and count of consecutive free blocks

questions.question: What is the purpose of a buffer cache in file system implementation? How does it improve performance?
keyPoints: Stores recently accessed disk blocks in main memory
Reduces disk I/O operations by serving requests from cache
Improves performance through temporal and spatial locality
Uses replacement algorithms like LRU when cache is full

questions.question: Explain the structure of a UNIX inode. How does it support files of different sizes?
keyPoints: Inode contains file metadata and data block pointers
Has 12 direct pointers for small files
Single indirect pointer for medium-sized files
Double and triple indirect pointers for large files
Enables efficient access for files of varying sizes

questions.question: What is file system mounting? Explain the concept of mount point.
keyPoints: Mounting integrates a file system into the existing directory tree
Mount point is the directory where the new filesystem is attached
Makes files and directories of mounted filesystem accessible
Operating system reads superblock during mount operation
Different operating systems support multiple filesystem types via VFS

questions.question: Compare linear list and hash table directory implementation approaches.
keyPoints: Linear list: simple implementation, O(n) search time, files added at end
Hash table: uses hash function for O(1) average lookup, handles collisions
Linear list better for small directories, hash table better for large directories
Hash table requires additional handling for collisions and table resizing

questions.question: Explain the concept of file sharing and protection in multi-user file systems.
keyPoints: File sharing allows multiple users to access files simultaneously
Protection mechanisms prevent unauthorized access
Access control: owner/group/others with read/write/execute permissions
File locking prevents concurrent write conflicts
Operating system maintains security attributes and access rights

questions.question: With the help of a diagram, explain the organization of the UNIX file system. Describe the role of superblock, inode, and data blocks in file system implementation.
keyPoints: Boot block contains bootstrap loader, superblock contains filesystem metadata
Inode array stores metadata and block pointers for all files
Data blocks store actual file contents
Inode structure: 12 direct, 1 single indirect, 1 double indirect, 1 triple indirect
Free block management through free block list
Directory entries map file names to inode numbers
Block group concept for better locality and recovery

questions.question: Explain the following allocation methods with examples: (a) Contiguous Allocation (b) Linked Allocation (c) Indexed Allocation. Compare them in terms of external fragmentation, internal fragmentation, and access time.
keyPoints: Contiguous: files get consecutive blocks, easy calculation, suffers external fragmentation
Linked: blocks linked via pointers anywhere on disk, eliminates external fragmentation
Indexed: separate index block holds pointers, fast random access, index overhead
Contiguous: best sequential and random access, worst external fragmentation
Linked: no external fragmentation but poor random access due to sequential traversal
Indexed: good random access but index block space overhead for small files
All have internal fragmentation in systems using fixed block sizes

questions.question: Describe the free space management techniques in detail. Explain how each technique handles allocation and deallocation of disk blocks, along with their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Bit Vector: simple, fast allocation O(1), requires extra memory proportional to disk size
Linked List: simple implementation, must traverse to find free blocks, O(n) allocation
Grouping: combines advantages, quick allocation of multiple blocks, complex implementation
Counting: efficient for contiguous allocations, tracking runs of free blocks
Trade-offs: memory usage vs allocation speed vs complexity
Modern systems often use combinations of these techniques

questions.question: Explain the different file access methods supported by operating systems. With suitable examples, describe how these access methods are implemented in various file allocation techniques.
keyPoints: Sequential access: read bytes in order, supported by all allocation methods
Direct/Random access: jump to specific block number without reading preceding blocks
Indexed access: use index block to locate blocks, efficient for large files
Contiguous naturally supports all access types efficiently
Linked allocation only supports sequential access efficiently
Indexed allocation supports efficient direct access via index block
Performance depends on allocation method and access pattern
